questions.text: I rarely experience the physiological markers of stress even in stressful situations.
I have changed my plans because I became aware that someone needed help, even when it was inconvenient.
I believe that careful planning is the foundation of meaningful accomplishment.
I think of time as something to be experienced rather than optimized.
I feel that my energy is best spent helping others reach their goals.
I think that the most equitable treatment of people's needs comes from those who can separate the emotional from the factual.
I feel that I am at my best when I make my own interests a top priority.
I can still feel the emotional residue of an argument hours or even days later.
I am the type of person who builds the roadmap.
I prefer shared responsibility to personal accountability for outcomes.
I hold the view that suffering, when recognized, creates a moral imperative that should be reflected in my actions, priorities, and choices.
I detect when someone is diplomatically declining an invitation.
I feel most confident when I am directing a process from start to finish.
I notice when someone offers a half-truth that is technically accurate but misleading in context.
I believe that life rewards those who show up consistently, day after day.
I prefer clear instructions over subtle hints about what is expected.
I feel that the highest form of contribution is quiet, unrecognized service.
I often feel flooded by the intensity of my own reactions.
